title: Born on Lenox Avenue
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Brooklyn in August 2013, John Carlos opens with his parents, a father born in Camden, South Carolina about sixty years after slavery and a mother who migrated from Cuba at seventeen, who married in New York City and raised their children on Lenox Avenue in Harlem.

title: A Good Show for the Money
summary: Carlos describes growing up between the Cotton Club and the Savoy Ballroom, hustling tips with a little song and dance and collecting silver dollars, a meeting with Fred Astaire who praised them for always giving a good show for the money, and his first sight of the white flight that emptied Harlem.

title: King Kong and Heroin
summary: He recalls the bootleg liquor called King Kong that sent drinkers leaping from rooftops, the arrival of heroin that disintegrated Harlem families and left mothers raising children alone, and how his own parents held together, his father a World War I veteran and shoemaker, his mother a domestic who cleaned doctors' offices through Harlem.

title: A Harlem Robin Hood
summary: He explains modeling himself on Robin Hood and the distinction between God's law and man's law, leading his friends to raid the frozen freight trains coming through the Yankee Stadium yards not for fat pockets but to stock the houses of neighbors who had nothing, while keeping it from his law-abiding parents.

title: To Swim the English Channel
summary: Carlos recounts hearing on the radio of a man swimming the English Channel and peppering his father with questions about sharks and tides, then learning of the Olympic Games and declaring he would be the first Black swimmer to represent the United States.

title: Because of the Color of Your Skin
summary: He relates the painful talk in which his father explained he could not train as a swimmer, not the river where friends drowned, not the crowded public pool, not a club, and finally the real reason, the color of his skin, recalling the white mothers at the High Bridge pool who called their children out of the water the moment he jumped in.

title: You Need to Talk to Him
summary: Carlos describes two Black detectives from the 32nd precinct, friends of his shoemaker father from weekend poker games attended by Jackie Robinson and other ballplayers, who suspected him in the freight-yard break-ins, and how his father told them to speak to the boy directly.

title: Johnny, You're Special
summary: He tells how the detectives caught him taking other boys' sneakers at a track meet, smacked him for smirking, but told him he had a talent as a runner because he had outrun the police, recalling that his mother too was a runner who fought to keep her purse, and gave him a number for the New York Pioneer Club.

title: Total Fatigue at the Armory
topic: First Race at the Pioneer Club
summary: Carlos recounts arriving at the New York Pioneer Club at thirteen or fourteen with no track gear, telling Joe Yancey he ran distance, and being thrown into 660s against older runners, leading the first laps before total fatigue dropped him, and overhearing on the cold walk home that everyone would soon know his name.

title: J C on My Buttocks
summary: He describes writing a big J and C on his sweatpants so crowds would chant his name, building a reputation though his high school had no track and the team trained running hallways and the East River walkways, all while he still wanted to be a swimmer.

title: A Pool That Was Not There
summary: Carlos tells how, as a self-described gangster kid barred from his school's track program, he was steered to Haaren High on a promise of a swim program that did not exist, was kept off its track team as the wrong sort of student, and finally transferred to the School of Machine and Metal Trades.

title: They're Singing About Us
summary: He recalls running at Machine and Metal Trades as a novice who had never won, breaking the record in his heat and finding the crowd had made up a song about the team, then the serious racing that happened outdoors after the meet at the 168th Street Armory, where he gave handicaps and raced the winners for crowds as large as those inside.

title: A Springboard for a Voice
summary: He describes coming to see track and field not as a way to collect medals but as a springboard, realizing the better he perfected his game the better he could speak and carry a message, never forgetting the drugs, the domestic work, the white flight, and the racism he had witnessed.

title: They Gave Us a Basketball Court
summary: Carlos argues that decades ago Black neighborhoods were given basketball courts where white areas had swimming pools, producing stars like Bill Russell and Elgin Baylor and the Rucker Park players who resisted letting pro money control their game, and laments that modern athletes care only about the dollar while leagues tolerate doping for bigger television contracts.

title: Shame on the Olympic Committee
summary: Carlos condemns Vladimir Putin barring gay athletes from the Sochi Games and faults the International Olympic Committee, recalling that in 1936 it let Adolf Hitler force young Jewish athletes off the German team, and says athletes today lack the courage to make statements because they fear repercussions more than they value what is right.

title: An Embargo on the Games
summary: Carlos explains he no longer agrees with boycotting the Olympics, equating it with an embargo that spares the government and hurts the people, since the officials still make their money while the spectators and athletes, the sacrificial lambs, are the ones hurt, recalling that Jimmy Carter was attacked for the 1980 boycott though he held life more valuable than Olympic medals.

title: A Grain of Sand
summary: Carlos calls Mexico City a spiritual thing, the first Games televised worldwide and in color and held in a developing nation, and recounts a vision from age seven or eight in which he stood on a podium and his upraised left hand changed the crowd's joy into venom and hatred, exactly as it would on the victory stand.

title: Stop the Train
summary: He frames the boycott debate as a train ride, building consensus among athletes then pulling the red lever to hang banners that let the world know, after which the crowd singing God bless America turns to firebombs hurled at the burning train, tells the latecomers who now want a brick at San Jose State to show him their burns, and says people stay silent only because they are afraid to offend the oppressor.

title: That's His Son
summary: Seeking religion to answer the voids in his life, Carlos found Abyssinian Baptist Church, where the elder Adam Clayton Powell resembled his father and one day handed the sermon to his fair-skinned son Adam Clayton Powell Junior, whose preaching was about the content of his heart, and reflects on his father's lessons about light-skinned people passing for a better standard of life.

title: Get on the Ships
summary: Carlos describes absorbing his heroes early, Marcus Garvey whose parades came up through Harlem and whose ships invited Black people back to Africa to find themselves, and Paul Robeson, the baritone, scholar, and movie star who had everything and risked it all rather than zip his lip, using his fame as a springboard to be a voice for those who could not speak.

title: Which One Is Malcolm X
summary: Carlos recalls hearing Malcolm X on the radio speaking the truth he had only heard from his father, going to the mosque on 116th Street, asking to walk with him from one location to the next to learn, and how he was supposed to be at the Audubon Ballroom the Sunday Malcolm was shot but had taken a road trip past West Point, racing back to the hospital only to find him gone.

title: God's First Lieutenant
summary: Carlos describes meeting Martin Luther King and being awestruck, raised to see King as God's first lieutenant, and learning King wanted to be not the number one figure for the Olympic boycott but second in command after Memphis, naming Harry Edwards and Ken Noel as organizers of the potential boycott of the 1968 Games sparked by a statement Tommie Smith made in Japan, while Carlos was off at East Texas State facing his own racism.

title: If You Go, We Go
summary: He recounts how a runner he met at a New Jersey meet helped get him a track scholarship to East Texas State, the coach promising no prejudice and a good life for his young daughter, and the choice between that scholarship and a New York housing-project apartment, settled when his wife told him that if he went, the family went together.

title: My Name Changed to Boy
summary: Carlos describes traveling south with a Jamaican-born running friend who insisted he was not Black, the pristine whites-only restroom beside the filthy colored one at the Dallas Fort Worth airport, the whites-only water fountain, and the coach who renamed him from John Carlos to boy and came close to a racial slur in front of his wife and daughter.

title: Above Being a Slave
summary: He describes a recently integrated school still ruled by an old slave-master attitude, a neighbor who screamed a slur at his small daughter for playing with a white child, and Carlos confronting the man knowing that if he killed him everything he stood for would go out the window, yet refusing to call his parents for money to leave.

title: As Great as Bobby Morrow
summary: Carlos tells how the coach warned him not to talk to white girls, how he reminded a coach's wife that his running paid the bills, and how a statement that he was as great as the Texas sprinter Bobby Morrow so offended people that a retraction was printed the next day.

title: A White Man on His Own Plane
topic: The Coach Picks Up a Hammer
summary: He remembers a white teammate he placed in the class of John Brown and Peter Norman, then the day before the Lone Star conference when the coach ordered him to run timed trials, Carlos refused and walked the track, and the coach came at him with a hammer and a slur before Carlos stripped off his uniform and ripped his name from the locker.

title: We Made a Bond
summary: Carlos describes calling his wife to pack while the coaches formed a delegation to take him to lunch, his insistence that they feed the whole team and not him alone, and his white teammate reminding him of the bond they had made to win the Lone Star championship, persuading him to stay.

title: A Ring or a Shoe With a Flake
summary: He recounts winning the conference and being given a tiny shoe charm while the mostly white football team got big rings, demanding equal rings for the track team, a bar in Austin that refused to serve him in the state capital he was representing, and deciding, with the hammer fresh in mind, that he had to leave East Texas.

title: Drop the Rock in the Lake
summary: Carlos tells how Harry Edwards phoned him to invite him to a meeting, his walk into the Americana Hotel lobby past the chandeliers to the SCLC suite where Andrew Young welcomed him, and King answering why he joined the Olympic Project for Human Rights with a parable of dropping a rock into a still lake, the waves rousing everything on the shore, a way to wake the world up nonviolently.

title: Stand for Those Who Cannot
summary: Carlos describes pushing his shades down to look for fear in King's eyes and asking why he would return to Memphis after threats on his life, King answering that he had to stand for those who would not and could not stand for themselves, a phrase that made Carlos's whole life come full circle and that he ties to the God-given icons people still talk about every day.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: Not for the Medal
summary: He explains that, unlike Kareem Abdul-Jabbar who could stay home for his studies unquestioned, if Carlos skipped the Games someone else would take his place, so he went not for a medal but to be represented on the victory stand, crediting God for placing Peter Norman, who alone among white athletes had the audacity to stand with them, on the podium.

title: Do You Believe in Human Rights
summary: Carlos recounts that when the boycott dissolved he and Tommie Smith resolved to make a statement, and with about twenty-five minutes before the ceremony he asked Peter Norman whether he believed in human rights, pinned on him an Olympic Project for Human Rights button thrown down by a white Harvard rower, and stresses that beyond the button Norman had the conviction never to flinch or denounce them through years of trials.

title: Take Their Picture in My Spot
summary: He tells how, learning that the San Jose State statue left Peter Norman out, he drove from Los Angeles to refuse his own likeness and phoned Norman in Sydney, who said he loved the idea but proposed his place be left empty so visitors from around the world could stand in his spot to support what they did.

title: From Hero to Neighborhood Bum
summary: Carlos recalls breaking the world record at the New York trials and owning the city, then thirty days after the demonstration the same paper calling him a neighborhood bum, his hospitalized father weeping over the headlines until Carlos reminded him that nobody knew John Carlos better than his own father, and the word trouble maker he came to wear proudly beside Gandhi, King, Robeson, and Mandela.

title: Beads for the Lynched
summary: Carlos walks through the symbols he and Smith chose, a black shirt over his USA jersey for shame at America, Smith's scarf for Black sacrifice, beads for those lynched in the South and thrown overboard on the Middle Passage, the shoes and stockinged feet for poverty, and the glove for five continents of people of color becoming a powerful force, then his thoughts on the victory stand that they could never shackle him again because he was a free man.

title: Bodies in the Square
summary: Carlos recounts the killing of close to two thousand students in a Mexico City square days before the Games, bodies burned and dumped at sea so the Games could go on, says his family and his God were his only support, and that the right-wing press tried to pit him against George Foreman, who waved the American flag yet became one of his dearest friends.

title: A Beacon for the World
summary: He contends the Republicans are rolling back the gains of the sixties and faults critics of Barack Obama, then describes the demonstration becoming a beacon that resonated in Cuba, Africa, Asia, and Italy because the button said human rights for all humanity, and how an eighty-foot picture of him at the Sydney stadium draws visitors from around the world.

title: A Thousand No Christmases
summary: Carlos says his only regret was being too young to better protect his family, naming the hurt of his first wife taking her own life, his children sacrificing at school because of their father, and the Christmases he could not give them, yet concluding that what he did in Mexico City was far greater than any of it because it was about all of us.

title: Become Part of the Kid
summary: He describes teaching by melting himself down to become part of a kid, like two candles blending, and the New York reunion of track athletes the night before where former students told him he had come to them not as someone over them but as part of them, confirming that the vision God showed him was coming true.

title: That's Me, the Hummingbird
summary: Carlos says the biggest sacrifice is losing a loved one and that personal sacrifice is irrelevant beside giving his all for those who come after, recalling two fellow Olympians at an airport telling him he had screwed up his life and would never work in America, and answering that like a hummingbird or a man who falls off a bridge and treads water, he did not go down, he stopped, and he would rise again.

title: I Can't Change History
topic: Those Who Want a Brick Now
summary: He recounts the people who tried to put him down now calling to be in the picture and wanting a brick, telling them he cannot rewrite history to add those who were not there, and closing on what he refuses to dwell on, the welfare and hungry house a friend quietly relieved with groceries, choosing instead to talk about coming together for the children, since everyone who did something for society, from John Brown to King, knew the sacrifice and was not deterred.
